stories.name: Patagonia
stories.headline: The billionaire who gave his company away.
stories.blurb: In September 2022, founder Yvon Chouinard transferred Patagonia's ownership to a trust and nonprofit, directing all non-reinvested profits — roughly $100 million a year — to fight the environmental crisis. The structure makes the planet, in his words, the company's only shareholder.

stories.name: ExxonMobil
stories.headline: Decades of climate research, then doubt.
stories.blurb: Internal Exxon scientists accurately modeled global warming as early as the late 1970s, according to a 2023 Science study reviewing the company's own documents. The company spent the following decades funding groups that publicly questioned that same consensus.

stories.name: Costco
stories.headline: The retailer that pays nearly double minimum wage.
stories.blurb: Costco's starting U.S. wage is around $20 an hour, with average hourly pay above $27 — well over the retail industry median. The company has publicly tied employee retention and turnover savings to that pay floor for more than a decade.

stories.name: Amazon
stories.headline: Twice the industry's serious-injury rate.
stories.blurb: A 2023 Strategic Organizing Center analysis of OSHA data found Amazon warehouse workers were injured at roughly twice the rate of the rest of the warehousing industry. The NLRB has separately issued multiple complaints against the company over union-organizing activity since 2022.

stories.name: Ben & Jerry's
stories.headline: An activist brand inside a $150B conglomerate.
stories.blurb: Ben & Jerry's independent board, preserved in its 2000 sale to Unilever, has publicly clashed with its parent over Israel/Palestine sales and other political stances. In 2024 the board sued Unilever, alleging the conglomerate silenced its advocacy.

stories.name: Chick-fil-A
stories.headline: The chicken chain that rewrote its giving.
stories.blurb: Chick-fil-A's WinShape Foundation drew years of scrutiny for donations to groups opposing same-sex marriage. In 2019 the company restructured its giving to focus on education, homelessness, and hunger, ending grants to the Salvation Army and Fellowship of Christian Athletes.

stories.name: Apple
stories.headline: Industry-leading audits, court-ordered store rules.
stories.blurb: Apple publishes one of the tech industry's most detailed supplier responsibility reports, auditing hundreds of factories each year. At the same time, U.S. and EU courts have ruled against the company's App Store policies, with a 2024 contempt finding that Apple defied an antitrust injunction over developer payment links.
